meanings.definition: To bend your body forward or downward, usually because you are picking something up, trying to fit into a small space, or because of tiredness, age, or sadness.
examples: He stooped down to pick up the coin from the floor.
The old man stooped slightly while walking with his stick.
She stooped to enter the small wooden door.
The boy stooped over his notebook and continued writing.

meanings.definition: Having a bent or hunched posture, usually because of age, weakness, or carrying heavy weight for a long time.
examples: The stooped gardener slowly watered the plants.
Years of hard work had made his shoulders look stooped.
She noticed a stooped figure standing near the gate.
The old teacher walked with a stooped back.

meanings.definition: Extremely dry, with very little rain or moisture; also used for something dull or lacking emotion or excitement.
examples: The desert remained hot and arid throughout the year.
Very few plants can survive in such an arid climate.
After weeks without rain, the land became arid and cracked.
His lecture sounded so arid that many students lost interest.

meanings.definition: A large group of birds, sheep, or animals gathered or moving together.
examples: A flock of birds flew across the evening sky.
The shepherd guided the flock toward the hills.
We saw a flock of pigeons near the railway station.
The flock moved together as if it were a single creature.

meanings.definition: To gather or move somewhere in large numbers, usually because something is attractive or important.
examples: Tourists flock to the beach during summer.
People flocked to the market before the festival.
Fans flocked outside the stadium to see the singer.
Students flocked to the library before exams.

meanings.definition: Soft fibers or small pieces of wool or cotton used for stuffing cushions, mattresses, or furniture.
examples: The pillow was filled with soft flock.
Old sofas often used flock as stuffing material.
The craftsman replaced the worn-out flock inside the cushion.
The toy was packed tightly with cotton flock.

meanings.definition: To cover a surface with tiny soft fibers to give it a velvet-like texture.
examples: The company flocked the wallpaper to give it a rich feel.
Some greeting cards are flocked for decoration.
The inside of the jewelry box was flocked with soft material.
The artist flocked the surface to create a textured design.

meanings.definition: Very hard and tiring work done continuously over a long period of time.
examples: Years of toil helped her build a successful business.
The farmers returned home after a day of toil in the fields.
Behind every achievement, there is often silent toil.
His hands showed signs of years of physical toil.

meanings.definition: To work extremely hard for a long time, especially with physical effort.
examples: The workers toiled under the hot sun all day.
She toiled for months to complete the project.
Miners toiled deep underground in difficult conditions.
He continued to toil despite feeling exhausted.

meanings.definition: Long, thin, and weak-looking, often in an awkward or fragile way.
examples: The young plant had a few spindly branches.
He walked on his spindly legs across the field.
The chair stood on four spindly wooden supports.
The puppy looked tiny with its spindly little legs.

meanings.definition: A downward bend or drooping shape caused by weight, weakness, or lack of support.
examples: There was a noticeable sag in the old mattress.
The roof developed a slight sag after the storm.
The rope showed a deep sag in the middle.
Years of use caused a sag in the sofa cushions.

meanings.definition: To sink, bend, or hang downward because of pressure, weight, weakness, or age.
examples: The shelf began to sag under the weight of the books.
His shoulders sagged with disappointment.
The old tent started to sag in the rain.
The branches sagged under the weight of the fruit.

meanings.definition: To lose strength, energy, confidence, or enthusiasm.
examples: Team spirit started to sag after repeated losses.
Her voice sagged as she spoke about the failure.
Sales began to sag during the winter season.
His motivation sagged after hearing the bad news.

meanings.definition: A long, sharp, squeaking sound usually made by old wood, doors, floors, or furniture when moved.
examples: She heard the creak of the wooden stairs at night.
The old door opened with a loud creak.
Every creak in the house made the child nervous.
The floorboards gave a soft creak as he walked.

meanings.definition: To make a sharp, squeaking sound when something old or heavy moves.
examples: The gate creaked loudly in the wind.
The wooden chair creaked as he sat down.
Her shoes creaked on the polished floor.
The ship creaked during the stormy night.

meanings.definition: To move or function with difficulty because of old age, strain, or weakness.
examples: He creaked out of bed after the long journey.
The old machine creaked into action.
Her knees creaked as she climbed the stairs.
The tired bus creaked along the mountain road.
